data.title: Lloyds Banking Group: NIM Expansion Powers A Solid Q3
data.description:
Shares of U.K. banking giant Lloyds Banking Group plc (NYSE:LYG) have had a strong year, returning over 70% with dividends. While ahead of European financials by over 30 points in that time, this strong performance basically only makes up for previous lost ground, with a three-year total return of ~40% roughly matching the wider European space.

I last covered Lloyds in July following Q2 results, rating it a “Buy,” as the stock was trading for less than 10x implied earnings even though its net interest margin (“NIM”) was close to hitting a bottom. With fresh quarterly results released today, now is a good time to review this thesis. I remain bullish on Lloyds and leave my previous rating unchanged.

A Pretty Strong Q3

In the bank's third quarter, Lloyds beat consensus on revenue, total operating expenses, asset impairment charges and net income. Profit attributable to ordinary shareholders also beat consensus by over 15%, landing at £1.21 billion, while the bank's return on tangible equity (“ROTE”) was 15.2%, in line with management's medium-term target of ~15%.

While Lloyds continues to experience headwinds to its NIM from deposits and mortgages, structural hedge income was a powerful tailwind, leading to modest margin expansion in Q3. Following Q2 results, I had expected NIM to contract for maybe one more quarter before stabilizing, so this expansion was encouraging. NIM also beat pre-results consensus by 2bps. As Lloyds generates around 75% of its revenue from net interest income (“NII”), NIM and earning-asset balances are key lines to monitor.

Starting with interest income trends, NIM was 2.95% in Q3, up 2bps sequentially and ahead of consensus by the same amount. Tracking the various headwinds and tailwinds to NIM has been key to the bullish argument here, as the latter was seen outweighing the former in the coming quarters. This now appears to be the case, although deposits and mortgage margins continue to be a drag.

In terms of the former, the latter stages of the tightening cycle saw Lloyds pass on more of the Bank of England's rate hikes to depositors, which is a normal part of the interest rate cycle. At the same time, depositors have been shifting balances into interest-bearing savings accounts from current accounts. This trend was still ongoing last quarter, with retail current account balances falling by around £1.1 billion and savings account balances rising by ~£2.8 billion.

As for mortgage margins, these have tightened due to various factors but, as with funding costs, there is probably a strong cyclical component. Although it may sound counterintuitive, lending margins often deteriorate when interest rates rise. Although gross asset yields are obviously rising too, remember that what matters is the spread. Currently, the average 5-year fixed-rate mortgage rate is somewhere around 4.4% including fees, versus a current 5-year swap rate of ~3.7%. Put in plainer English, mortgages are being written at a relatively thin spread compared to more profitable back-book loans, hurting NIM. Collectively, these issues were a 7bps headwind to Lloyds' NIM last quarter.

On the flip side, a major component of the bullish case here is structural hedge income. For new readers, the structural hedge is essentially how Lloyds manages the potential interest income volatility associated with non-maturing deposits like retail current accounts. Unlike U.S. banks, European banks typically use interest rate swaps rather than bonds to achieve this, but you can still imagine this as being like a bond portfolio that earns a margin for the bank, smoothing NII over time.

As previous hedges written at lower yields mature and are replaced, Lloyds' portfolio yield has been rising, aiding the bank's NIM and NII. This provided a 10bps uplift to NIM last quarter, more than offsetting the headwinds mentioned above. Given prevailing swap rates and the forward curve, this should be an ongoing tailwind for the bank assuming an orderly easing cycle.

Earning-asset balances also rose slightly last quarter. Small and medium-sized business lending was quite weak, with total balances falling by around 3% sequentially, but this was more than offset by growth in retail mortgages, unsecured consumer and corporate & institutional balances. Overall, average earning-asset balances rose £2 billion sequentially to £451 billion, virtually in line with consensus. With NIM expanding and earning-asset balances growing modestly, NII also rose, increasing 2.5% sequentially to £3.23 billion. This was slightly ahead of pre-results consensus, driven by stronger than expected NIM.

Lloyds performed well elsewhere too, with non-interest income of £1.43 billion beating consensus by ~3%. Turning to expenses, underlying costs of £2.92 billion rose by just over 2% year-on-year. While this was virtually in line with consensus, lower remediation charges technically drove a small beat here overall. Keeping costs contained will obviously be key to translating revenue growth into earnings growth going forward. With revenue and total operating expenses both modestly ahead of expectations, Lloyds' 53.4% cost/income ratio also represented a beat, as did pre-provision income, which at ~£2.03 billion was ~4% ahead of consensus.

Credit charges continue to be impressively contained. While difficult to forecast quarter-to-quarter, Q3 impairment charges of £172 million beat consensus by around £100 million, boosting the bank's overall earnings. Digging deeper, updated macroeconomic assumptions did not drive this line like it did in Q2, so that charge is basically the underlying impairment. At around 15bps of total loans, this was a strong performance, with Lloyds comfortably on track to meet guidance of <20bps for the full year.

Lloyds' mortgage book is secured with an average LTV of 43%, providing a strong cushion if house prices fall. In terms of particular areas of market concern, commercial real estate lending balances of ~£9 billion only represent around 2% of Lloyds' total loan book, and around half of this is residential related. The average LTV of Lloyds CRE book was also strong at around 45%, with over 90% of CRE balances below a 70% LTV.

Shares Still Look Cheap

While income statement drivers have formed one plank of my ongoing bullishness with Lloyds, capital returns potential and the stock's valuation have been the others.

On the former, Lloyds continues to target roughly 175bps of capital generation in 2024, which it can return to shareholders via dividends and share buybacks. Having generated 132bps over the first nine months, the bank is on track to meet this. Given ~£220 billion in risk-weighted assets, that implies roughly £3.9 billion for the full year — a figure I view as attractive given the current market capitalization is around £38.4 billion. Put simply, Lloyds can return around 10% to investors simply through dividends and share repurchases.

Being a bank, “normalized” earnings are arguably more important than what is made in any given year, as this could be under/overstated depending on prevailing macro conditions. Importantly, Lloyds continues to target a >15% ROTE by 2026, basically in line with what it posted last quarter (~15.2%). Given ongoing NIM expansion, I view this as roughly achievable.

At the same time, capital generation is also still seen heading to over 200bps by 2026, implying ongoing double-digit shareholder yield potential (i.e., dividends plus buybacks). With the stock still trading for around 1.2x tangible book value, the implied price-earnings ratio likewise remains in the single-digit region (at ~8x). As such, Lloyds continues to look like a solid value play and even offers a margin of safety should profitability land a little below management's target. I remain bullish on the bank and stick with my previous “Buy” rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CTO Realty Growth: Cheaply Valued With High Dividend Yield And Growth Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis For CTO

CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (NYSE:CTO) is a high dividend-yield stock with incredible growth potential, resulting from its strategic locations in blooming regions in the U.S. It's also cheaply valued compared to other diversified REITs.

Introduction: CTO Stock

CTO Realty Growth is a retail-centered REIT with a market cap of $583.55M, making it a small-cap stock.

They specialize in open-air shopping centers in the South, giving CTOs exposure to high-quality retail in expanding markets.

Its primary locations are in the southeast and southwest, massive markets that have been consistently growing the fastest in terms of population in the US. Occupying a position in growing markets while at the same time being a smaller company shows potential for solid growth in the coming years.

However, potential alone is not sufficient; CTO also needs to be a strong business and have solid financials.

The perfect mix is, thus, being a strong company with solid business fundamentals while being located in an environment that experiences growth.

CTO holds a 93% occupancy and 95% leased occupancy, with very solid brands and well-diversified businesses.

Their portfolio is predominantly located in Texas, Florida, and Georgia, having 4.6M sf and $1.3B in investments in the last five years.

Overall, CTO is ideally placed to grow its operations alongside southern states' growing demographic and economic development.

Their high-quality brands provide the added benefit of enhanced resilience and the possibility of further expanding CTO Realty Growth portfolio.

CTO Stock Earnings

CTO Realty Growth Inc. currently produces $62.32M of Funds from Operations.

It appears to have slowed down in the last few years, with a recent growth trend from mid-2023 onwards.

As a young and small REIT, there is massive room for continued growth, especially as the location of its properties is ideal for catching strong tailwinds from a booming southern economy and population.

FFO estimates and surprises are a mixed bag for CTO. Although it has beat consensus during the last four quarters, during 2023 and early 2024, it experienced a slowing down of earnings with stagnating growth, followed by a slight decrease in Q2 2024.

The overall situation is expected to improve in 2025.

REITs are understandably slower to build on their earnings, as building, buying, or renovating properties and then finding adequate tenants take longer than increasing product sales.

Nonetheless, I will keep my eye on this trend. As a smaller company, sustainable growth should be the main focus at this stage.

I rate this as neutral. I don't think there are enough signs to worry about, but we are treading a fine line between bullish and bearish.

Balance Sheet For CTO

Let's start with the short term:

The Current Ratio for CTO is pretty good, with current assets outweighing current liabilities at 1.587; this shows the company can manage most present situations.

Long-term, CTO Realty Growth uses the typical laddered debt, with most of its $484 million in debt maturing in 2027 and 2028, for a total of $350 million for those two years.

I would personally like the debt to be a little more spaced out, with maybe another $100 million maturing in 2029, to better distribute risks and avoid putting so much pressure on those two years.

I consider it not ideal, but it is not that dangerous either. Nonetheless, CTO should be prepared to walk into 2027 and 2028 with a robust financial position to avoid having to refinance at worse terms.

On the other hand, Financial Debt to Equity has decreased since its peak in Q3 of 2023.

Continuing this trend should be a goal for CTO and one of the things that I will continue to monitor.

REITs can be debt-heavy, as their primary way of making money involves intensive use of capital. So far, the ratio is regular.

Overall, CTO realty Growth is not a balance sheet fortress but still has an okay financial situation.

Margins And Profits

The margins for CTO stock have shown a solid capacity for profit-making, with a comfortable Gross Profit Margin of 73.17%, a Levered FCF Margin of 47.89%, a Net Income Margin of 14.52%, and a ROA of 1.66%.

The numbers make a compelling case for a company in the early stages of its cycle with plenty of potential for improving its margins as it builds a moat and refines its strategy and operations while building meaningful relations and economic goodwill.

Economic goodwill should not be confused with accounting goodwill. The first, as defined by Warren Buffet in his 1983 letter, is the sum of subjective advantages a company has, like a stellar reputation, well-located real state, strong connections with its broader business environment, and other unquantifiable but crucial factors.

The latter, accounting goodwill, is the excess paid for acquiring another company above its Net Asset Value.

I wanted to make the distinction to illustrate how these margins will evolve as the company grows and matures, with some of them improving not only as a result of capital investment but also from building on economic goodwill.

Still, as a young and small-cap company, the profit margins for CTO Realty Growth should be a test of whether its business model is viable and whether management has made sound decisions so far in the present economic environment.

The real test will come in the future. It will measure how the company can continue improving its margins or maintaining them in the face of changing market conditions and higher amounts of capital to deploy.

CTO Dividend

Here, CTO shines, with a high dividend yield of 7.81%, 11 years of dividend growth, and a comfy margin of safety between its FFO per share of $ 2.657 and the dividend per share of $1.52.

But on a deeper look, there are some warning signs that I will particularly continue to monitor.

The FFO per share appears to be capped below $3.0 for the last seven years, while the dividend per share has steadily climbed, particularly in the previous four years.

This really begs the question: How long will this trend continue? Unless the FFO can break from its $3.0 earnings ceiling, further dividend increases could be limited to the bare minimum or even stopped altogether.

As CTO, it's a stock that many investors will look into or buy because of the attractive dividend yield; I think it's essential to ponder this question.

I believe a small company in a growing market has plenty of potential to increase its FFO. Yet, we should also watch for signs of this actually beginning to materialize.

Alpine Income Property Trust.

CTO has also taken a step further in diversifying its holdings by seeding and managing another REIT.

This other REIT is Alpine Income Property Trust Inc. (NYSE:PINE), which has a market cap of $284.06M

PINE leases tenants from massive, well-established companies like Walmart, Best Buy, and Lowe.

This provides CTO with indirect exposure to the benefits of risks that come from titanic tenants. Even though a small REIT will have problems negotiating very favorable terms with such giants, it benefits from their security and stability.

In other words, I expect PINE to have a more stable but probably smaller growth in its FFO, resulting from the previously mentioned imbalance in the negotiations.

Both companies can also benefit from exchanges in know-how and some strategic planning, but the pressure to do this is on CTO's management as they manage PINE.

Alpine Income Property has demonstrated growth in its AFFO, surpassing expectations and establishing itself as a growing REIT.

This could be bullish evidence of the quality of management and good business acumen for CTO as their seeded and managed REIT has so far proven to be a strong stock on its own.

Risks And Competition

Any smaller company has an inherent risk that bigger and more mature ones do not have; not only do they have less capital, contacts, and experience to bear on the market, but we, as analysts, have a shorter track record to analyze.

Currently the main states for CTO are experiencing high growth, and any market with high growth is bound to bring heavy competition eventually.

Valuation For CTO Realty Growth

For the valuation, I used my classic, comparable company analysis.

The stocks I used are diversified REITs with similar market caps.

CTO Realty Growth stock won on the Price to FFO and Price to Adjusted FFO (the first one tied for the first place)

It shows good revenue growth, but I don't consider this too much of a win for CTO. It is currently in a mixed situation with respect to growth, and it still needs to prove it can continue growing despite previous stagnation.

The dividend yield was close, but CTO still has a clear lead and one very attractive dividend.

Finally, on Price to Book, it was a close second.

Overall, this paints CTO as a stock with a high dividend yield, relatively cheap for the funds it's currently generating, and with assets that are also quite cheap.

My main philosophy is based on getting good deals all the time; from my analysis, the CTO appears to provide great value for its current price.

Conclusion

CTO Realty Growth is a very well-located REIT that holds a very attractive portfolio and has very robust growth possibilities thanks to the thriving states in which it is located. This high dividend yield also offers excellent value for its price and is very attractive compared to others. I rate it as a BUY.

data.title: Are You Looking For Data To Support Your Views?
data.description:
The S&P 500 finished down a hair to end its streak of 30 trading sessions without a back-to-back decline, which was one of the longest stretches on record going back to 1928. The implications are bullish for the year ahead, as previous occurrences of 30 or more, which are not many, found the index higher one year later seven out of eight times. More impressive is that the Nasdaq Composite’s streak of 33 sessions is still intact, with all previous 12 occurrences resulting in the Nasdaq being higher one year later. This is the forest, which investors should not lose sight of when focusing on the trees from one day to the next.

Several of those trees were smoldering after the close of disappointing news. Consumers are clearly getting tired of paying $7-$8 for a cup of coffee at Starbucks, as the company’s sales fell short of estimates and management withdrew guidance for 2025. McDonald’s sales will undoubtedly suffer to some extent after some 49 customers in ten different states were infected by an E. coli outbreak after consuming a Quarter Pounder. Lastly, analog chipmaker Texas Instruments gave investors a disappointing outlook after beating earnings estimates. More reasons to worry include rising bond yields, the war in the Middle East, a potentially larger budget deficit next year, and the prospect that the Fed may not lower rates as rapidly as investors expect.

This list of concerns replaces the ones we had three months ago, which replaced the ones we had three months before that, and so on. There are always concerns or things to worry about, but when the market ignores them, we should too. We may think they are important, but what, we think, doesn’t matter. The greatest lesson I ever learned as an investor was to stop investing based on what, I thought, should happen or what was important. Instead, focus on what was most likely to happen and how the market responds to incoming data. Every time I found myself looking for commentary or data to support my pre-determined views about the markets or economy, whether they be bullish or bearish, my investment strategy failed to produce the desired result.

As an example, if you have a dire outlook for the US economy today, and you came across the chart below, as I did yesterday, it is easy to draw a very negative conclusion about the health of the consumer that affirms your dire view. We are starting to see an increase in delinquencies on credit card and auto loan debt, as some lower-income households run out of excess savings and struggle to service revolving debt at historically high-interest rates.

If we dig further into consumer balance sheets in aggregate, and we recognize that it is not the amount of debt that dictates a consumer’s health as much as the consumer’s ability to service it, the dire outlook doesn’t look so dire. Service payments by households on outstanding debt as a percentage of disposable income (after tax) has risen to 11.5%, but that is still below pre-pandemic levels and the lowest level we have seen since the mid-1990s. This is why retail sales, as well as overall consumer spending, continues to grow at a near 3% annualized rate on an inflation-adjusted basis. Therefore, we have two pictures of consumer health. One is rather dire, while the other looks relatively good. From a neutral perspective, the second is a lot more important than the first, which is why the steady pace of economic growth has sustained through 2024.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: It Is Hard To Argue Against Math, Time To Buy
data.description:
My previous article on Medical Properties Trust Inc. (NYSE:MPW) was issued back in May 2024, when I plotted a compelling equity story for investors to potentially open a minor high-risk and high reward position. Namely, it was clear that the combination of MPW's success on the asset disposal end with a progressing situation with Steward should eventually help MPW emerge out of the crisis without a significant equity dilution event.

Since the publication of my prior article, the following things have happened:

  • MPW has issued its Q2 2024 earnings deck, which reveals some important insights.
  • Management has cut the dividend once again, going from $0.15 per share to $0.08 per share (quarterly basis).
  • An external investigation has refuted accounting misconduct related to claims by short-sellers.
  • MPW has sold 11 additional facilities, receiving $86 million of cash.
  • MPW has finally reached an agreement (or clarity) with Steward in relation to access and use of the properties, which have been subject to irregular (and in some cases completely none) cash lease payments due to Steward's financial difficulties.
  • MPW has delivered ~2.5% in total returns, thus suggesting that the upside potential is still there.

Now, while many of my followers have probably noticed that my core investment strategy revolves around finding truly durable and defensive yield-bearing securities that are also subject to at least inflation-level growth, in some instances I can make tactical investments as long as the following criteria are met:

  • The investment produces dividends.
  • It has an asymmetric risk and reward profile (with upside much greater than the potential risk).
  • There is a room left in my overall portfolio for incremental high-risk positions (i.e., the high-risk high reward bucket should not consume more than 5% of the total exposure, and it all should not be concentrated in a single security).

Looking at MPW now and considering the aforementioned data points, in my view, the investment case is extremely attractive. In fact, by assessing the underlying financials deeper, it is even hard to arrive at a situation in which MPW would continue to see its market cap drop in a tangible fashion.

Let me explain.

Thesis review

My thesis (or overall argumentation) could be captured by five distinct aspects.

First, even after the recent dividend cut, MPW's FWD yield stands at 6.7%, which offers an opportunity for income-seeking investors to open a tactical position without sacrificing the current income component. More importantly, the FWD P/FFO remains very depressed at 5.3x, thereby providing a favorable basis from which to capture outsized returns at the moment, when MPW delivers the first tangible (and incremental) positive data points. The health care equity REIT average P/FFO multiple stands at 14.4x. Obviously, I am not making a case that we should expect MPW to converge back to the sector average, but it just gives a solid perspective on how deeply depressed the current multiple is. Ultimately, if MPW manages to release Steward-related properties and in the process suffers no meaningful equity dilution, I would not be surprised if in 2–3 years the multiple comes closer to the 10x mark.

Second, in Q2 2024 data, we could already see the first material benefits from MPW's asset monetization activity, which has helped management to fully neutralize 2024 borrowings, and take care of some minor chunk of 2025 maturities.

For example, if we compare Q1 2024 to Q2 2024, we will see how the debt to adjusted EBITDA has come down from 8.8x to 8.0x, with the adjusted interest coverage ratio improving from 2.5x to 2.6x. Plus, we have to keep in mind that these metrics do not include the proceeds from a general acute care hospital in Arizona of $160 million and the recently announced $86 million from additional property sales.

In essence, considering the success of the YTD 2024 asset divestiture strategy in combination with management's (Edward Aldag, CEO, see below), the 2025 refinancing risks seem to be very limited:

We remain focused on accelerating debt paydown and have several available levers to create additional liquidity, comfortably satisfying our expected maturities in 2025 and beyond.

Third, the FFO generation in Q2 was quite robust despite the fact that MPW did not receive the lion's share of cash income from Steward's properties, which together account for 17.5% of MPW's asset base. Even with these difficulties, MPW managed to register FFO of $0.23 per share, which based on the recently cut dividend, implies that MPW is able to retain circa $366 million of FFO (on an annualized basis). This is a significant portion of capital in the context of the forthcoming debt maturities. It also provides a decent margin of safety in case there would be additional struggles on the Steward asset monetization end (e.g., theoretically, the Q2 Steward related revenues could decrease further from 7.5%).

Fourth, as mentioned above, MPW has finally arrived at a more concrete solution in relation to the assets (23 properties) that are currently leased to Steward. The key essence is this:

  • MPW is expected to receive annualized cash rental payments of ~$160 million starting from Q4 2026.
  • The first cash rent payments are expected to occur in Q1 2025, reaching about half of the stabilized rent level by the end of 2025.
  • Cash rent payments will start for 15 separate properties in 2024.

For example, if we take half of Q4 2026 stabilized cash rental payments, express them on a quarterly basis and apply it to the Q2 2024 results after adjusting for what has been received from Steward in Q2, we would get an immediate uptick in FFO generation of roughly 9%.

This is not even taking into account the organic growth component, which stems from the embedded rent escalators, positive leasing spreads and organic growth projects (granted, both of the last two aspects are not significant, but they still contribute to higher FFO).

Fifth, we have to appreciate the fact that the Fed has made a 50 basis points of a cut in base rates, which obviously was not reflected in Q2 financials. We should expect the first benefits of this in Q3 as the variable rate credit facilities will become less expensive to serve.

On top of this, the FOMC dot plot curve below reflects a favorable path of gradual interest rate normalization, which will come in very handy for MPW when monetizing incremental parts of its portfolio and conducting the forthcoming refinancings.

All in all, given the elements above, it would be fair to say that MPW is in a position from which its FFO generation will only improve as the Steward properties are shifted to tenants, which actually can serve lease payments. Plus, we could expect slightly lower interest costs and some additional support on the FFO generation from organic growth avenues.

All of this should eventually trigger a notable repricing.

Having said that, we have to also be cognizant of the potential risks that could come from other struggling tenants (the refinancing and Steward risks are largely solved as described above). In such a case, I would not be surprised if MPW's market capitalization level continued to suffer (go lower).

However, there are some important elements of risk mitigation in place, which we should consider:

  • MPW has a FFO payout of ~33%, which leaves ample margin of safety to absorb some decline in the FFO.
  • MPW is likely to increase its FFO generation from here, which could then mean that a suffering tenant simply offsets the growth component without decreasing the existing levels.
  • The total portfolio EBITDARM rent coverage levels have been increasing for the third quarter in a row, landing at 2.2x in Q2. Importantly, Prospect, which was speculated to be second in line after Steward, has actually managed to significantly improve its rent coverage from 0.9x in Q1 2024 to 1.3x in Q2 2024.

The bottom line

Medical Properties Trust presents a truly asymmetric risk and reward profile with its downside largely protected by already absorbed damages from non-cash generating Steward properties and multiple items, which should drive the FFO generation up going forward (e.g., ability to release Steward related properties). The depressed multiple coupled with ~36% short interest provides a strong upside potential as soon as MPW delivers first tangible points of improvement (e.g., collecting cash from Steward properties, reduced interest costs from lower SOFR, attractive portfolio monetization, etc.).

In a nutshell, the case looks mathematically justified even if we assume a very pessimistic scenario of MPW not receiving any cash from its Steward assets. Meanwhile, investors can earn a fairly attractive yield while waiting for the thesis to play out.

I have decided to allocate ~1.2% of my total portfolio asset base into MPW.

data.title: DIVO Continues To Outperform High-Yielding Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
The Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (NYSEARCA:DIVO) is having a strong year compared to many of the popular higher-yielding call option ETFs. DIVO utilizes a tactical covered call strategy on individual positions rather than a specific index. While other high-yielding call option ETFs are producing high single-digit to low double-digit yields, a lot of the upside appreciation is getting capped to produce immediate income. DIVO continues to become a popular ETF with $3.69 billion in assets under management (AuM) as it's a bridge between income and appreciation. DIVO is appealing to me because it takes a strategy I am utilizing in my individual positions and morphed it into an ETF with 44 holdings. DIVO holds many quality positions that are not in my portfolio, which also adds a layer of diversification to my holdings. I think that DIVO will continue to outperform many high-yielding funds from a capital appreciation standpoint and provide investors with a level of income that can rival the current level of risk-free assets. I am bullish on DIVO and think that it will follow the market higher.

Following up on my previous article about DIVO

Since my previous article was published in April (can be read here), DIVO has appreciated by 9.39% while having a total return of 11.61% after the dividends are accounted for. DIVO continues to follow the market higher as the S&P 500 has appreciated by 14.70% over this period. DIVO has been up over 20% over the past year, and I am following up with a new article as we head into earnings season. I think that big tech is going to deliver for investors in Q3 and set the stage for a strong Q4. Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) are both part of DIVO's top-10 holdings, in addition to other quality companies such as UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and Caterpillar (CAT). Now that we're entering a lower-rate environment, I think that there will be a lot of capital coming back into the market as companies expand their earnings from a better business environment. DIVO could continue attracting investors looking for a hybrid approach to appreciation and income.

Risks to investing in DIVO

While DIVO is an ETF, it still has a concentrated portfolio of 44 holdings, which could make it more volatile than investing in an S&P 500 index fund or a total market fund. In the event that we see a correction or the market turns lower, DIVO could experience elevated losses compared to the market. DIVO also utilizes a tactical covered call strategy on its individual positions to try and generate an additional 2-4% in call option premiums when an underlying position is demonstrating strength or implied volatility. This is great for income, but if a position is showing strength, then the management team risks the chance of capping upside potential as they are writing call options into upside movement. Investors are at risk of underperforming the market and not generating as much income as other covered call ETFs that continuously write call options each month. DIVO is subject to traditional market dynamics, and in the event of a pullback, DIVO doesn't implement a hedging strategy, so it is likely to follow the market lower unless its holdings are outperforming the market.

DIVO has outperformed Many Covered Call ETFs and one of the largest dividend-focused equity ETFs in 2024

Some investors want large amounts of income, while others are looking for a hybrid approach. DIVO isn't structured to compete with ETFs such as JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) or the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD) from an income perspective. DIVO has a dual focus on generating income and appreciation. DIVO invests in positions that fund managers feel will be appreciated in value while providing an opportunity to generate worthwhile premiums by utilizing a call option strategy. The fund is able to harness the dividends that are paid from its underlying holdings and generate additional value by producing option income. This allows DIVO to outperform many income ETFs that utilize options while proving a hybrid ETF that allows investors to benefit from appreciating markets while generating income.

When I look at many of the high-yielding call option ETFs, including JEPQ, XYLD, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD), Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI), and NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) in addition to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), DIVO is outperforming all of them on an appreciation basis. Since the beginning of the year, DIVO has increased by 13.23%, while the next closest fund in the group appreciated by 11.82%. SCHD, an equity ETF focused on companies that generate a large dividend yield but don't utilize a covered call strategy, increased by 10.84% over this period. XYLD and QQQI appreciated by 5.04% and 4.72%. When I look at the forward yield that these funds are producing, SCHD came in at 3.46%, which is the lowest, followed by DIVO at 4.41%. In my opinion, DIVO is approaching $4 billion in AUM because it can outperform funds like SCHD in appreciation and yield while giving investors an equity-focused ETF that can rival risk-free investments from a yield perspective.

DIVO's top-10 holdings account for 51.31% of its portfolio. The Amplify Samsung SOFR ETF has a portfolio weight of 4.88%, but since it's an ETF, I am going to leave it out of this section. The top-9 positions in DIVO represent 46.43% of the portfolio. DIVO is likely to move higher if these positions do well. Currently, these 9 positions have a collective forward P/E based on 2024 earnings of 23.60. Over the next 2 years, these companies will produce 19.45% ($3.39) in average earnings growth. This places their forward P/E based on 2026 earnings at 19.55x. I think this is relatively low, considering many companies trade at more than 25 times forward projections. I think that these companies will drive DIVO higher over time as the market appreciates. These companies are some of the largest companies in the market, and I don't foresee that positions such as AAPL or MSFT will be sold off.

Why I believe the market will continue higher after the election and into 2025

Every time since 1948 that the United States has experienced a recession, unemployment increased by more than 1% into the recession. Many individuals get scared when things are going well, and we are getting a great setup heading into 2025. While a recession is inevitable, I don't believe one is on the horizon anytime soon. Unemployment never increased by 1% since hitting a low of 3.4% in April of 2023. In July of 2024, unemployment rose to 4.3% and started declining. The United States has had 9 consecutive quarters of GDP growth, and the last quarter came in with an annualized increase of 3%. When the economy is growing, and the job market is strong, it's hard to have a recession. The recent monetary policy from the Fed provided its first rate cut since they started raising rates in 2022. The Fed is expected to continue cutting and finish somewhere in the 3-4% range at the end of 2025. As the Fed lowers rates, the cost of capital will decline, making it easier for businesses to expand. I think that the combination of rate cuts, GDP growth, and a strong labor market is a recipe for the bull market to continue.

Unemployment and recessions

I looked at the largest holdings in the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF Shares (VTI), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG). These 3 funds have the same top-10 holdings, except SPYG has both classes of Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) shares. If these companies do well, the market should continue higher as they represent 29.84% of the total market index, and 34.72% of the S&P 500. Growth drives earnings, and the combination of these companies are not lacking in this department. As a group, these companies are trading at 42.91 times 2024 forward earnings. Over the next 2 years, they're expected to grow earnings by 50.26% ($4.18) on average, which places their forward P/E based on 2026 earnings at 27.49 times. The business landscape looks like it's going to be bullish for the market, and as these companies are expected to expand earnings by 50% over the next 2 years, I think it will drive the market and DIVO higher.

Conclusion

DIVO is an interesting ETF as it delivers capital appreciation and income for investors. If you're looking to maximize either investment objective, then DIVO may not be the best investment product for your objectives. DIVO is worth considering if you're looking for a hybrid ETF that can deliver on both objectives. As the Fed takes rates lower, I believe that more capital will flow into the market as there are fewer investment options available to most investors. DIVO will be an attractive alternative as the yield resembles what a money market account generates, with the ability to generate double-digit returns. I think that there is a strong case to be made that the bull market continues and that many of the holdings within DIVO do well in a lower-rate environment. I think that we could see DIVO continue to appreciate and increase its dividend as its underlying holdings return more capital to shareholders next year.

data.title: PulteGroup: Margins A Concern In A Mixed Q3 (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of PulteGroup (NYSE:PHM) plunged 7% on Tuesday after reporting disappointing Q3 earnings. The Federal Reserve’s 50bp rate cut has not provided the hoped-for boost to the housing sector with mortgage rates actually higher since then, given the rise in long-term bond yields. I have been consistently bullish on Pulte, and even after this drop, shares have slightly outperformed the market since my July analysis, gaining 6% and reaching my $145 target before Q3 results. Given updated results, now is a good time to see if investors should become more cautious on PHM. I see valuation as less compelling and am moving to a hold.

In the company’s third quarter, Pulte actually beat estimates with EPS of $3.35, $0.22 ahead of consensus, as revenue grew by over 7% to $4.3 billion. Earnings rose 16% from last year, and it registered 7,924 closings, up 12% from last year. Frankly, Pulte, aided by its diversity across geographies and price point, has navigated the stagnation in the housing market over the past year quite well. However, within this report, there were some warning signs that have damped sentiment.

As noted above, PHM grew deliveries, but it did so by delivering out of its backlog. The pace of orders was a bit more disappointing. New orders were down 0.5% from last year at 7,031. Over the summer, some homebuyers may have pushed off buying a home, hoping that Fed rate cuts would reduce mortgage rates. Instead after falling during Q3, mortgage rates have begun rising again as long-term bond yields rise, now back above 6.6% this week.

Because of the rise in mortgage rates post-Fed, incentives have “remained elevated” given this rate environment as affordability remains “a tough hurdle.” As it is, incentives have been higher. In Q3, incentives were 7% of selling price, up 70bps. Pulte has maintained a $548k average sales price, similar to last year. However, builders have engaged in other activities, like “buying down” the mortgage rate to make purchases affordable.

From a buyer’s perspective, this is the same as lowering the price as monthly mortgage payments are lower. The builder prefers this approach to cutting the list price as it is easier to reduce the level of incentives if the market strengthens than to actually raise list prices. Because of increased incentives, PHM reported a reported a gross margin of 28.8% in Q3, which was down 70bps from last year. In addition to more incentives, sales were skewed more to the West, a lower margin region.

In addition to a lower gross margin, SG&A rose to 9.4% of home sales revenue, up 30bps from last year, as it invests in expanding its sales activity. Additionally, Pulte has been relatively aggressive in expanding its footprint. This added cost could reduce margins if the market were to weaken. So far this year, Pulte has had a $850 million growth in inventory to about $12.6 billion. There was $1.4 billion of land spend in Q3.

Pulte has spent $3.7 billion through three quarters on land, and it expects to invest about $5.1 billion this year. Consequentially, it controls 245k lots from 223k last year, up 10%. Importantly, most of this growth is via options rather than outright purchases, reducing its financial risk.

Still, it is notable that Pulte has increased its lot count even as its backlog is down 1500 units from last year to 12,089 units, totaling $7.7 billion. This land activity points to an optimism that orders will begin to rebound; however, we did not see it in Q3 with orders down slightly from last year.

Now, this backlog has functioned exactly as it is supposed to. PHM has been able to increase deliveries and earnings by delivering out of its backlog. Its backlog has declined by just 11% over the past year, so PHM can continue to operate at its current production level for several years before running out of backlog. Still at some point, we will want to see net new orders be at the same level as its closings to stabilize the backlog level, especially to justify land investments.

Unfortunately, based on comments in the earnings call, this appears unlikely in the near future. Management does not expect a clean read on housing demand until the spring. As noted above, mortgage rates have risen again, weighing on demand. On top of this, recent hurricanes may weigh on near-term demand in Florida. As it is, the Florida market has been a bit of an underperformer recently.

Overall closings were up double digits in Q3, but Florida closings were flat at 1,984. The West rose nearly 25% to 1,530 with Texas and the Midwest also rising substantially, driving the companywide gain. Hurricanes Helene and Milton likely have delayed some activity, even if they do not permanently reduce demand for Florida real estate. With two back-to-back storms, October was likely a soft month, and it will take some time for normalcy to return. This is significant for Pulte because a quarter of its backlog sits in Florida.

These various negatives help to explain why Pulte shares reacted so poorly to results. Still, anytime I find myself getting bearish on the company, there are positive fundamentals worth reiterating. First, at its current run-rate PHM is a very cash generative business. It has earned $1 billion of free cash flow this year even with its inventory build. Holding inventory flat, PHM is an over $2.3 billion free cash flow business.

Additionally while it has added some risk via its land spending, its balance sheet is pristine. Pulte has just 12.3% debt to capital. Including $1.5 billion of cash, net debt to capital is just 1.4%. PHM has virtually no net debt. Given strong free cash flow, it did $320 million of buybacks in Q3, and as a result, there has been 5.1% share count reduction over the past year.

Finally, at a macro level, as I have discussed previously, America has too little housing. You can see below how little construction occurred in the 2010s. This has left us at least 3 million units short of equilibrium. This lack of housing is a reason I believe prices have held in well during the rate hiking cycle and why even if rates stay higher for longer, I do not expect a significant downturn in pricing. Even if PHM is not a buy, these factors make clear to me it is not a sell.

Based on Q3 results, I believe PHM remains on track to generate $13.25-$13.50 in EPS this year, as I forecast in July. That said, I am less optimistic on 2025 given what is likely to be a slower start to the year amid uncertainty in Florida and higher mortgages rates. I had previously seen at least $14.50 in EPS next year. Given about 4-5% share count reduction, this year’s earnings would translate to about $14 in EPS in 2025. I view $14, rather than $14.50, as a more reasonable base case as I am hesitant to estimate net income rises given these uncertainties and recent margin pressure.

I still view ~10x earnings as the appropriate multiple given the cyclicality and more aggressive land posture. As such, I am reducing my price target to $140. That represents just ~5% upside. Accordingly, I am moving shares down to a “hold” from a “buy.” Unless mortgage rates reverse lower toward 6%, I would wait for shares to pull back into the mid $120’s before buying Pulte.

data.title: AT&T Vs. Verizon: Which Big Dividend Stock Wins In A Rate-Cut Market?
data.description:
Internet is the new utility

With earnings coming up/out for the two biggest cellular and internet data providers, it seems like an ideal time to compare the two and see which one is superior, particularly for yield and valuation. Verizon (NYSE:VZ) and AT&T (NYSE:T) have been compared to junk bonds, which have more volatility. They pay out pretty massive dividends compared to the rest of the stock market and if you catch them at the right time, they can provide a total return better than any corporate bonds or preferred stocks. However, grab them at the wrong time and the downward price destruction can wipe out all the expected yield for the year as well.

Having last covered AT&T back in July of 2023, the total return since that period on that dip to $14 has been market beating:

AT&T has also been the winner in total return year to date, but is it the better value with all things considered? I have not covered Verizon individually in the past, but am excited to comprehensively compare the two in this piece.

I will break down each of the steps I normally go through when doing a comparative analysis for a buy and pick a winner. I will mark the winner in each category or issue a tie. At the end, we'll tally up the points and see which one is the better selection.

A note about rate sensitivity in a Trump VS. Harris administration

Before we begin, I'd also like to point out that both of these companies are thought of as a fixed income proxy. They aren't going anywhere and although both are categorized as communication services, they should really be re-labeled as utilities. Internet and cellular service is something that none of us can live without just like water, electricity and waste service.

With rates already falling and the election on the horizon, these could be particularly hot stocks, especially in a Trump presidency as he has expressed his thoughts about being more active in expressing his opinion about rate cuts whereas the Biden administration barely uttered a peep. I consistently like to remind everyone that with Trump being a real estate developer and having a circle of constituents in that same vein, they primarily view rates as the leading factor in creating more cash flow, that is their go-to thought process and solution for economic stagnation, get a lower interest rate.

We should see rate cuts in either scenario, a Trump or a Harris administration, I just personally feel we will see them faster in a Trump administration.

Revenue exposure for both

Data courtesy of FactSet

AT&T
REVENUE SEGMENT % of revenue
Wireless services 68.60%
Wireline services 27.82%
Pan Americas mixed telco 3.21%
other 0.37%
VZ
REVENUE SEGMENT % of revenue
Wireless services 75.86%
Telecommunication Carrier Services 22.48%
Other 1.66%

Year to date total returns

Total return wise, AT&T has been the clear winner on a year to date basis but also the stock that has been thrashed the hardest in respect to the negative percentage off of it's all time highs.

A lot of this of course has to do with the poor decision to try to integrate Warner Brothers Discovery (WBD) into its business [AT&T] which destroyed more value than it created. Some of the stock's value was sloughed off as a stock dividend to investors [I personally sold my shares and rolled them back into AT&T], but a lot of it was just poor management and a load of debt.

Verizon held up better, remaining a true cellular and internet provider. Now that AT&T is back to its roots, these are two of what is becoming a triopoly along with T-Mobile (TMUS). As we compare these two behemoth companies, we'll find that the economic trends mimic each other in many ways.

Dividend comparisons side by side

Verizon still has the larger dividend, not cutting it as AT&T did post Warner Brothers spin, and has longer-term growth because of it. I expect both to be able to pay and raise slightly from here on out if they stick to their business rather than try to diversify into non-regulated ones to chase growth.

"Winner Verizon"

Comparing the dividends to free cash flow

  • Verizon free cash flow payout ratio trailing year: 80.5%
  • AT&T free cash flow coverage trailing year: 38%

AT&T with the lower dividend payout per share has an advantage in this position. They have much better dividend coverage at current based on their free cash flow per share. AT&T CEO John Stankey recently noted at the Goldman Sachs Communacopia conference that their 3 year turnaround is coming to fruition, they're growing free cash flow and paying down debt.

Stankey attributes a lot of this to getting ahead of competition in replacing legacy assets, investing more than competition in new fiber assets.

"Winner AT&T"

Comparing the balance sheets

In these two charts I compare a few of my must know balance sheet items.

  • Shares outstanding
  • Long term debt
  • Cash and short term investments
  • Number of turns LT debt is compared to EBITDA [credit quality]

Zooming in on shares outstanding, the float on AT&T has increased only slightly more than Verizon with both about flat.

Long term debt is now higher for Verizon at $150.76 Billion, a moniker that AT&T was branded with when analyzing this sector prior who now has less debt at $137.33 Billion by comparison.

LT debt is now 3.029 X EBITDA for AT&T and it is 3.75 X for Verizon.

Cash and short-term investments stand at $6.72 Billion for AT&T and $2.065 Billion for Verizon.

AT&T is the winner here with a clearly superior balance sheet.

"Winner AT&T"

Comparing the short term reflexive patterns

In the case of which is demonstrating better growth in profitability metrics versus price appreciation, Verizon now appears undervalued on this metric with price in the negative over the past 3 years whilst free cash flow and revenue are positive. The main crunch to their numbers has been the decreasing in GAAP EPS.

AT&T is harder to measure with this data due to the decoupling of Warner Brothers Discovery, but we will still give this round to Verizon nonetheless.

"Winner Verizon"

Earnings expectations

AT&T FY 2024-2025 expected EPS growth rate: 3.6%

Verizon FY 2024-2025 expected EPS growth rate: 3%

Let's be blunt, both of these have paltry earnings growth and basically grow alongside inflation, if at all. Analysts are expecting a tad more growth out of AT&T. They win this round but we should be glad that both have expectations for next year that are not negative.

"Winner AT&T"

Valuations

In this model, I use the classic Warren Buffett "Owner Earnings" model. Similar to discounted cash flow, except this takes into account taxes, interest and stock-based compensation. This model uses GAAP net income as it's base profitability metric and then adds back depreciation and amortization. Finally, TTM CAPEX is deducted.

This is all discounted by the risk-free rate using the 10-year Treasury. Buffett argued that if a company could predictably grow earnings from the point of entry into the future, then an investment in the evaluated stock would be better than an investment in a risk-free alternative like the 10-year Treasury since that is a fixed return versus a growing one for said company. Currently, the risk-free rate sits around 4.2%, and I will use this as my discount rate.

AT&T Owner Earnings valuation

AT&T amount in millions usd
net income 12725
deprecation and amortization 16656
CAPEX 17366
Owner earnings 12015
  • $12,015 discounted at 4.2%= $286,071 fair market cap
  • Divided by 7,170 shares outstanding = $286,071/7,170= $39.89 share
  • Selling at 53% of fair value.

Verizon Owner Earnings valuation

VERIZON amount in millions usd
net income 11252
deprecation and amortization 17854
CAPEX 16768
Owner earnings 12338
  • $12,338 discounted at 4.2%= $293,761 fair market cap
  • Divided by 4,209 shares outstanding =$69.79 fair value
  • Selling at 59% fair value.

Versus The Graham Number

Since both of these are companies that trade at a very low value in respect to their book values, I also like to compare these two using the Graham Number.

This value metric described in The Intelligent Investor by Benjamin Graham takes into account both the asset value and earnings power in creating a price model. Not many companies are relevantly priced with this metric as intellectual property can now get much higher ROA than tangible assets. Communications, financials and some industrials still rely on tangible assets almost solely and this metric still remains a good measure in those sectors.

The equation is simply the square root of (EPS X Book Value X 22.5) where the goal is to buy a stock where the P/E ratio X the P/B ratio does not exceed 22.5:

AT&T
BV 14.69
2024 expected EPS 2.2
GRAHAM NUMBER $26.96
Selling at % of fair value

79.7%
VZ
BV 22.85
2024 expected EPS 4.57
GRAHAM NUMBER $48.47
Selling at % of fair value 85.61%

On both valuation metrics, Owner Earnings and The Graham Number, AT&T is also the clear winner in this category.

"Winner AT&T"

Earnings week

Verizon Q3 earnings 10/22/2024:

Full transcript on Seeking Alpha:

EPS of $1.19 beats by $0.01 | Revenue of $33.33B (-0.02% Y/Y) misses by $90.06M

The market had a negative -5% reaction to the top line miss by Verizon.

Here are some notes from CEO Hans Vestberg:

Initially, in some of the states, we had challenges, especially with power, but I think that our team did a fantastic job to get our networks up pretty quickly. So, again, this is things that are happening constantly around the world right now in hurricanes and natural disasters. For us, building the networks, as we are always doing, is extremely important to see the resilience and on the network during this time.

Looking at the financial, growing 2.7% in the wireless service revenue is great. I'm also proud to report the biggest profit EBITDA in the history of Verizon, $2.5 billion in the quarter, which is really good and its multi-factors.

On the operational side, we started getting an even better balance on the postpaid side. We were 239,000 net adds positive.

All in all the takeaway from Vestberg's opening remarks were better profitability and cash flow and top line issues were largely avoided with some mentions of natural disasters and hurricanes as a possible excuse for top line results.

AT&T earnings expectations 10/23/2024:

With 11 revisions downward in the last 90 days, there shouldn't be an extremely high bar to pass and a surprise beat also becomes more likely when rolling into a print with low expectations.

As noted in the Verizon call, power outages and natural disasters could be affecting internet and cell usage. On an LTE basis, both companies have a completely saturated map. However when looking at 5G, AT&T has a much larger investment in that network and higher concentration and overall signal density in regions recently hit by hurricanes, thus we may see a bigger top-line hit to AT&T than Verizon:

AT&T 5G coverage estimate

Verizon 5G coverage estimate

The winner of the versus battle is AT&T

In my estimation, AT&T wins as the better stock. They win in my following head-to-head categories:

  • Dividend coverage by free cash flow.
  • Balance sheet strength.
  • 2025 Earnings Expectations.
  • Valuation on both owner earnings and the Graham Number.

Verizon won in two categories.

  • Dividend yield
  • Reflexive profitability patterns.

Summary

Both of these are high-yield utility-like stocks. I equally weighted both a few years ago and have decent returns. As a person who has zero bonds in his portfolio, these fill the bond proxy void for me. AT&T is the buy for now with a clear turnaround being executed by CEO John Stankey.

data.title: DoubleVerify: Multiple Drivers For Long-Term Growth Despite Near-Term Headwinds
data.description:
Investment thesis

DoubleVerify (NYSE:DV) shares have halved in value this year due to near-term headwinds from reduced ad spending by some larger customers. While overall growth has been decelerating over the past few quarters, some of its newer product offerings are performing well, leading management to anticipate a re-acceleration of growth in Q4. I expect the company to maintain growth rates in the high teens in the medium term and see significant potential for adjusted EBITDA margins to rise to around 34%, compared to the 31% projected for FY24. However, with a Price/FCF multiple of 17.4, I find the valuation less compelling, especially given the elevated levels of stock-based compensation ("SBC"). As a result, I rate DV shares as a Hold.

Key insights and financial highlights

DV's Measurement offering guarantees that customers' ads are placed alongside fraud-free, appropriate and safe content. Then, through the Activation product, customers can further leverage DV's platform to enhance the ROI generated on their ad spend. Notably, the Activation product is priced three times higher than the Measurement offering. Revenue from these two products is based on a fixed fee per impression, making it proportional to the total number of impressions served. These segments show impressive gross and net revenue retention metrics for its advertiser customers of over 95% and 120% respectively.

The company’s third and most recent product offering focuses on providing data analytics that validate the quality of ad inventory owned by its supply-side customers. This product is offered as a SaaS solution, primarily to retailers and CTV publishers. The Retail media advertising market represents a large future growth opportunity for the company, as it is expected to grow at a 12% CAGR, reaching close to $140 billion by 2026.

DV's shares have halved in value so far this, significantly underperforming versus other ad tech peers. This weakness can be mainly attributed to management lowering full-year guidance in Q1, implying a significant deceleration in growth versus the prior year. Management noted that revenue growth was negatively affected by reduced spending from six of its large customers, particularly related to the usage of its Authentic Brand Suitability product. This impacted the Activation segment, which grew only 12% year over year in Q2, as illustrated in the slide above, taken from the Q2 Earnings Presentation. Importantly, the decreased ad spend from these customers stemmed from their internal challenges, rather than any issues related to DV.

Revenue growth for the Measurement and Supply side products continued to be solid, demonstrating year-over-year growth rates of 22% and 26% respectively. However, as Activation represents a majority of the overall revenue, total revenue in Q2 only grew 16.5%. Despite this, adjusted EBITDA was strong at $47 million, reflecting a margin of 30%, which was the same as the year ago period.

Expectations for Q3 and beyond

Management's guidance for Q3 anticipates 17% year-over-year growth, as shown above. For FY24, guidance of $671 million at the midpoint translates to expected revenue of $205 million for Q4, implying an acceleration in year-over-year growth to 19%. This Q4 outlook appears particularly strong, considering the continued headwinds in its Activation segment and the potential for reduced ad volume from advertisers who may cut back due to temporarily elevated CPMs driven by political spending related to the elections.

One of the key growth drivers for the business looking ahead comes from its social segment, which includes all the leading social media platforms. Here, social related Measurement revenue grew 44% year over year in Q2, with international revenue up 29%. Activation revenue was correspondingly up 30%, helped also by DV's Scibids-AI product which it acquired last year, that optimizes the real-time bidding process for advertisers.

Furthermore, revenue growth for the Measurement and Activation segments should be aided by the recent closure of Oracle's Ad business, Moat, which was a competitor to DV. Though DV has already won several of Moat's customers, it is likely that a significant impact from this will only be seen later next year, as these customers are gradually upsold to DV's product offerings.

Meanwhile, CTV represents another growth driver that investors should consider, as DV's CTV Measurement volume grew 55% year over year in Q2. With almost all major platforms such as Amazon (AMZN) Prime Video, Netflix (NFLX) and Disney (DIS) introducing ad-supported tiers, ad inventory is expected to increase substantially, leading to lower CPMs, which in turn should lead to higher ad volumes. As DV's revenue is based on the volume of ads it serves, it is expected to be a major beneficiary of this trend.

Given the presence of these growth drivers, if the macroeconomy does not deteriorate significantly, I expect the company to continue to demonstrate high-teen growth rates in the medium term. When digging into margins, guidance for FY24 calls for adjusted EBITDA margins of 31% at the midpoint. In see plenty of room for margins to move higher heading into next year while incremental revenue should increasingly flow to the bottom line, given the operating leverage in the business. My confidence stems from the fact that DV's closest competitor, Integral Ad Science (IAS), which I covered recently, operates on a similar business model and has gross margins which are 400 basis points lower (79% versus DV's at 83%). Yet, IAS achieves adjusted EBITDA margins close to 34%. Given the outlook for growth that I see for DV, I expect it to reduce the margin gap to IAS during FY25, and I will be closely monitoring this in the upcoming earnings calls.

Given the continued FCF generation expected in the coming quarters, along with the company's strong balance sheet comprising $339 million in cash and no debt, I expect a significant portion of cash to be deployed towards share repurchases to offset the dilutive impact from SBC. Management also has plenty of flexibility with regard to continuing to look at accelerating growth through M&A like its acquisitions of Scibids in August 2023 and Meetrics in August 2021.

Valuation

With shares trading at $17, DoubleVerify has a market cap of $2.89 billion, assuming 170 million shares outstanding. Its enterprise value stands at $2.55 billion, factoring in a net cash position of $339 million. Based on the latest full year adjusted EBITDA guidance provided by management of $210 million, shares are valued at an EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple of 12.2. I estimate the company's FCF conversion from adjusted EBITDA to be close to 80% when adjusting for tax payments of close to $20 million and full year capex spend of $24 million, assuming no impact from changes in working capital. I arrive at an estimate for FCF in FY24 of $166 million, which translates to a Price/FCF valuation of 17.4.

Taking my valuation a step further, if I estimate DV's FCF generation potential assuming it reaches adjusted EBITDA margins close to 34% in FY24, the Price/FCF multiple drops to 15.9, based on a FCF estimate of $182 million. While this valuation appears reasonable given its double-digit growth and strong profitability, I will show why I find the valuation less compelling.

IAS, which is demonstrating slightly slower growth rates but considerably higher margins, is valued at a Price/FCF multiple of 14.3. This suggests that DV shares trade at a premium to IAS, which is partially justified by DV's faster growth and stronger balance sheet, while IAS has a small amount of net debt.

However, it's important to note that annual SBC expenses remain high, with an annual run rate of approximately $90 million, as shown above. This represents over 13.4% of the revenue for FY24, significantly higher than IAS’s SBC expenses, which are around 11% of its expected revenue for the same period. On a GAAP basis, the valuation becomes significantly higher, leading me to conclude that the shares are not cheap at these levels.

Risks to consider

Competition

With Oracle's Moat exiting the digital advertising verification and measurement market, DV and IAS are now the two primary players. While DV has outperformed IAS over the past three years, the short report by Spruce Point last year claims that IAS offers more competitive products, particularly noting its significantly larger portfolio of patents.

Both companies heavily depend on partnerships with major demand-side platforms ("DSPs") like The Trade Desk (TTD), which could potentially provide similar services within its own platform. Additionally, the rise of AI poses a threat, as self-service platforms powered by AI become increasingly viable alternatives.

Overhang from large owners selling shares

Providence Equity Partners holds nearly 15% of the total shares outstanding according to DV's latest annual report, and has been aggressively selling their stake since the IPO in 2021. Their most recent sale, totaling $56 million, occurred at the end of last year when the share price was at $30. This ongoing selling pressure likely dampens investor sentiment regarding the company's stock.

Macroeconomic headwinds

As mentioned earlier, election-related media spending could create a minor headwind for Q3 and Q4, as advertisers may cut back on ad volume due to temporarily elevated CPMs. Additionally, entering a period of economic weakness could lead to lower revenue from reduced impressions. However, since a portion of revenue is tied to fixed CPMs, the business is expected to demonstrate some resilience.

Conclusion

I see several growth drivers for DV that point to a promising outlook. Additionally, there is significant potential for margin improvement due to the operating leverage in the business. However, the valuation is less compelling due to the high level of SBC, which leads me to remain on the sidelines for now.

data.title: Third Avenue Value Fund Q3 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Fellow Shareholders,

For the three months ended September 30th, 2024, the Third Avenue Value Fund (the "Fund") returned 1.26%, as compared to the MSCI World Index[1], which returned 6.46%, and the MSCI World Value Index[2], which returned 9.73%.

The Fund benefited from positive quarterly performance contributions by Capstone Copper (OTCPK:CSCCF), Lazard (LAZ), easyJet (OTCQX:EJTTF), Comerica (CMA) and CK Hutchison Holdings (OTCPK:CKHUY), among others. The list of positive contributions comprised a wide range of geographic and industry exposures. Positive influences on this group of investments were, in our view, primarily idiosyncratic to the individual companies and were not tied to any obvious common thematic developments.

On the other hand, negative performance contributions resulted from Tidewater (TDW), Valaris (VAL), Subsea 7 (OTCPK:SUBCY), Horiba (OTCPK:HRIBF), and BMW (OTCPK:BMWYY). Thematically speaking, offshore energy services companies represented the three largest detractors from performance during the quarter. In recent years, the Fund's investments in the offshore energy services industry have been among the largest contributors to Fund performance and we continue to perceive the prospects for owners of high-quality offshore service assets to be very bright. Typically, the beginnings of the end of an industrial cycle for offshore energy services companies would be marked by increasing orders of new assets slated to enter the industry after construction. Today, there is virtually no newbuild order book for the types of assets operated Valaris and Tidewater. There are a handful of new orders for the type operated by Subsea 7, though they are smaller, less capable, and not likely to compete directly with industry bellwethers, such as Subsea 7 and peers. Rates earned by offshore services companies and their competitors still need to move materially higher in order to justify new asset orders and, even in that scenario, it takes years for an asset to be constructed and delivered.

Furthermore, the Fund's two automotive companies, BMW and Mercedes-Benz Group (OTCPK:MBGAF), both detracted from performance even while both remain highly profitable, well run, extremely well-capitalized, and inexplicably cheap, in our view. We believe that there is very attractive value in several automotive industry OEM's, which is reflected throughout each section of this letter, and we are eager to own them at current valuations.

Not Dead, Just Playing Possum

It is possible that value investing is genuinely dead, as a few have surmised. To our knowledge, the coroner has not yet issued a final report, but the evidence is mounting. In the collection of evidence, we continue to note the valuation chasm between cheap and expensive companies in U.S. equity markets. This phenomenon is not unprecedented, but the current iteration grew gapingly large and seems unusually persistent.

There are many ways to evaluate the valuation relationship between cheap and expensive stocks, within various indices and within specific industries, though every version of the analysis we have conducted or seen suggests very strongly that the ten-year period from 2011 to 2021 was associated with relentlessly elevating multiples assigned to the most expensive segment of the U.S. equity market. Meanwhile, multiples assigned to the least expensive segment of the U.S. equity market seemed to develop rigor mortis. The chart below depicts the historical price-to-earnings[4] multiple assigned to the most expensive quartile of the S&P 500, as well as the multiple assigned to the least expensive quartile, with the shaded area depicting the spread between the two. By 2021, cheap versus expensive valuation spreads in the U.S. eventually reached levels not seen in many decades. The chasm shrunk in 2022 but grew again in 2023 and 2024 and remains quite wide by historical standards.

Further, though subtle, it is notable that the chart below also shows that multiples assigned to the least expensive quartile actually declined in the decade from 2014 to 2024. Perhaps not surprisingly, the extensive duration of this episode, defined by cheap stocks becoming cheaper and valuation spreads growing ever-larger, has caused many market participants to ponder whether equity markets are genuinely fundamentally "broken." At the core, the "broken equity markets" and "death of value" investigations share quite a bit in common.

In the context of this valuation conversation, several potential contributing factors have been cited, including, i) a dramatic shift of assets to passive strategies, ii) the ability of individual investors to organize on the internet, which is a potentially powerful facilitator of what Charles MacKay dubbed The Madness of Crowds, and iii) hyper-low interest rates. We wrote a white paper in 2023 explaining why we don't believe interest rates offer a legitimate explanation, so won't belabor that here.

Regarding non-professional investors organizing on the internet, we do have some riveting anecdotes of rebellious, intentionally economically-irrational, coordinated behavior. Still, we simply don't have enough information to analyze the overall contribution of these collusions to the unusual valuation spreads within equity markets. A significant majority of the U.S. equity market is held outside of any types of regulated funds and lies in the hands of individuals, corporations, and institutions. In our experience, it is very hard to gain insight into the collective activity and motivations of that very large and disparate group of investors.

On the other hand, it does seem eminently reasonable to conclude that the trillions of dollars of capital that have been redeemed from actively managed funds and reallocated to passive strategies in recent years has likely had a significant influence on the U.S. equity market's price discovery process. Actively managed funds experiencing net outflows, which describes the vast majority, have been compelled to sell securities over time to meet net redemptions. According to Investment Company Institute ("ICI"), only 28% of long-term mutual fund complexes saw positive net flows in 2023. Presumably, the securities sold include the highest-conviction investment ideas of the active managers, whereby the selling contributes to the equity market's price discovery process in a manner completely disconnected from the active portfolio manager's fundamental view. In other words, price discovery is driven by transactions, not by a fundamental view. The ability of active portfolio managers to drive the price discovery process can be severely stunted if they are grappling with consistent outflows, which constitutes a threat to some of the underlying precepts of market efficiency.

Although correlation does not necessarily mean causality, it is not at all surprising that "cheap" versus "expensive," and large-cap versus small-cap, valuation spreads standout as particularly large within the U.S. equity market, as compared to non-U.S. markets. The U.S. is, after all, where active to passive portfolio flows have been exceptionally strong. According to the ICI, "from 2014 through 2023, index domestic equity mutual funds and ETFs received $2.5 trillion in net new cash and reinvested dividends, while actively managed domestic equity mutual funds experienced net outflows of $2.6 trillion (including reinvested dividends)". Flows of capital to actively managed domestic equity ETFs have been positive over that period but are extremely small in the broader context.

To conclude, we find it difficult to imagine that there has not been a material impact on price-discovery as a consequence of shifting a material percentage of the entire U.S. equity market from fundamental investment strategies to passive investment strategies, which ignore the relationships of security prices to corporate fundamentals by mandate. The entire raison d'etre of passive strategies is to rely upon a price discovery process conducted by active equity market participants, who are purported to digest fundamental information rapidly and efficiently. However, it seems there is good reason to be fearful that the collective ability of active managers to drive that process has been stunted by outflows and that the weight of flows into passive strategies has been effectively usurping the price discovery process. And while we tend to agree with industry observers who assert that it is not necessarily problematic for passive strategies to manage more capital than active strategies, we do believe that the process of getting to that point, which has entailed massive capital flows from active funds to passive funds, has likely impacted the price discovery process in a meaningful way.

Where Have All the Possums Gone?

Once a part of the structural foundation of equity markets, the community of value-oriented boutiques has been significantly impacted by these distortive phenomena. It is indeed a business strategy offering little insulation from these challenges. A number of firms have closed, while some others have adjusted their strategies to be more in keeping with the present investing zeitgeist. It also seems that very few of the remaining value-focused firms have been of a mind to grow staff and train the next generation of young enterprising value investors. Having observed this value community attrition for a period of years, you can imagine my surprise when, in the midst of considering whether I was personally a relic of bygone era, I came across the following passage from a well-known global auto analyst at one of the world's largest investment banks:

"Whilst BMW's new cash accounting highlights almost EUR 70 per share in industrial net cash and, hence, a very deep value investment, the lack of a short-term "dream" narrative and a lack of value investors leaves BMW often overlooked."

We only use a small amount of hyperbole in saying that this analyst has captured the current state of equity investing in a single sentence. Rephrasing his words; "Obvious substantial undervaluation doesn't matter because there are no value investors left to care and all the non-value investors care about is a "short-term dream narrative." Factual fundamental information concerning business value is not a focus, nor is its relationship to security prices." Maybe he is correct that we price-conscious, value-oriented investors are among the last of the Mohicans. Maybe the fundamental value investing community no longer oversees an asset base that is powerful enough to facilitate price discovery and reconcile undervaluation. Or, perhaps more accurately, the fundamental value investing community is currently unable to participate in the price discovery process in a manner broadly consistent with its fundamental views. So, maybe a tree doesn't make a sound if it falls in the woods and there is nobody to hear it?
